## [27:16 - 30:19]

But the key, as I said, it's probably a setup, but the principle there is extremely important, whether you're in person selling or trying to do this before you launch a product, let your prospects tell you what would it take for you to buy this. And then people will give you their reasons for buying. Probably that was one of the best sales presentations I ever saw. The worst was one, in the entire history of the world perhaps, was one that I was involved with. When being a writer, we ran that ad announcing a direct marketing agency that will guarantee to beat your best ad. And we were all writers, you know, Claude Hopkins Fanatics, and came time to go out and meet the clients who were potential clients who were responding to this ad. So they sent me, among other writers, we had no idea how to sell in person. We knew a lot about selling it on paper and multiplied salesmanship. But there's a personal dynamic that's a whole other set, a whole other skill set. And unlike what Fred Herman did, which I saw years later, I went to one potential client. It was a collection agency and he was thinking of hiring a direct marketing agency to maybe help him with his collection letters. So I went in, he kept us waiting for about half an hour, I and another person from the company. And I went in, I was the lead person from the company. So I said, oh, hello. And after the pleasantries being introduced, he kind of threw his pen down, he folded his arms and said, hey, sell me. And so I proceeded then to tell him all about direct marketing, how it's so measurable and how we love Claude Hopkins, which he never heard of. I just went around chasing my tail for about a half hour telling him all the things that I thought would impress him. And then he kind of had a phone call and he said, let me think about all this and I'll get back to you guys. And we were out of there right after that. I committed the worst mistake I could have. What I should have said was, well, I don't know that I can sell you, just like Fred did. But let me ask, why are we here? Let me just sort of play doctor, if you will, and take your temperature and diagnose the situation before I may prescribe something. And somebody who was at the seminar said research is the foundation. So let me just give a couple of pointers on research. First point is cast a wide net. When you're researching something, you really shouldn't, and I made this mistake many times myself, if I thought at the very beginning of the assignment that I had a great idea for a headline, I would write it down. And I saw what would happen very often is that I would almost start researching in the direction of that headline. So I would not recommend that as a practice. I think if you come up with a good headline, you can write it down, save it for later. But in the research stage, you want to be wide open to anything that you might possibly discover. You want to interview star salespeople, especially because they know how to sell that particular product. You want to interview the product manager. You'd like to interview the head of the company or division. You want to interview the customers, always very good people to interview. You want to study the product literature. You want to really load up on competitive products and their literature, their direct mail packages and so on. And when I have taken on assignments in the past, my advice to the client is please just back up the truck. You cannot send me too much information. Because I can go through it fairly quickly and see what's needed. And very often, you'll find a nugget of research in some place that you never thought you'd find it, which is just the kernel of a blockbuster ad. So be very thorough in your research and don't start writing. You really have to separate the writing process into separate stages and we'll get into more of that later. But the first stage, you just pure research. You're just not looking to solve any problem just yet. You just want to be wide open. And then that's easy. I mean, it's just like reading and talking with people. You don't have that pressure on you of solving the problem immediately. You're just going to see how other people like this product, don't like it, how could it be improved. There'll be some questions. And one of the pieces that you'll receive and one of the examples of my work is a little newsletter combination brochure that I did when I went out on my own.

## [33:23 - 36:28]

It's called Salesmanship in Advertising. And it's about eight pages, I think. It shows some more of my samples and the questions that I'd like to ask before you, during the research process, all about what is the product, who uses it, who are the 20%, who buy 80% of the product. Questions like that. And I won't run through them all here, but they're in that literature. So your goal is to gather at least seven times the information that you can possibly use. This was something that John Capels personally said to me. That's the advice he got from his copy chief many, many years ago. I shouldn't say his copy chief because John was not a copy chief to anybody at BBDO. He was much more of just a very nice guy. He was around and just did special projects on his own. He was his own one little department. He was such a revered figure in advertising. But he would come over to where the copy writers were and I'll copyright a bullpen and try to help us and read our copy and give advice. He was a very, very wonderful man. But he was not a copy chief. He never served that way at BBDO that I know of. Certainly not for me. He used the questions in the salesmanship and advertising brochures. I mentioned a couple of them are why is a product made this way? Why is it better than alternative solutions? Who buys it? What are the hot buttons? The lingo, the buzzwords, main problems. Where is John Calton? John Calton is so good at doing this. Is he around? Maybe I'll be back later. Okay. He'll get it spoken in the back. He'll appreciate that you told us all that. Thank you, Joe. But John Calton is very good at writing about golfers. He'll pick up their lingo and their slang and whatever words they like to use, their problems that they have out on the greens so that when other golfers read the ad, this is written by a golfer who really knows where I come from. That's very important in any field to get to know the buzzwords and the lingo that your potential prospects use. Also in your research, here's some of the things that you're looking for. We want to be able to fill the slots of the persuasion equation. We want to find and all the research, what problems is this product designed to solve if it's more than one? Just make a whole list. What elements could I use to make the promise even more strong? What proof elements are they? That's a biggie you must look for and really make almost your top priority. What are the dramatic proof elements that will let somebody see that magical combination? Powerful promise? Sandwich with very compelling proof? There's so little of that why French women don't get fat. That's a proof and promise all in the same short title. That's a beautiful piece of marketing. You don't have to have it that short. You want to know if I make this bold promise, how can I sandwich it together, solder it at the hip with compelling proof so that people just don't write it off as more hype?

## [36:28 - 39:28]

Always remember that the proof is like the goal that your prospects know to look for. Everybody has very sensitive BS meters and if you're just going to crank out a lot of adjectives about how super fantastic something is, it's just going to get thrown away. I think I'll share some in publication that I write the bullets. But make a little collection, a little folder full of great bullets that are so intriguing and provoke such curiosity that you can take the same framework of that little point and just substitute your promise into, in the body copy of your ad. You're looking for headline material and you're looking for anything interesting. Sometimes something will be in a totally different category of what you usually look for, the proof elements of promise, but it's just intensely interesting. Just make note of that as well when you're doing your research. Here's a basic way how I take my research. I just type up a lot of notes and kind of the sequence that I'm just going through. If it's a book, I'll read the book, every single page of the book. In fact, I learned this very well from Gene Schwartz. It was my first assignment for Rodale and filling their books that Gene had written a piece before me. I didn't want to look at his. I didn't want to be overly influenced by what he had done or depressed because he usually wrote such great copy and I had to compete with it. When I got done with my first draft, I took a look finally at his bullets. Let me see what he came up with. I got really depressed because he had done such a better job of research. He was phenomenal on his research. I said, the only way I'm going to have a chance of competing with Gene is if I figure out what he's done to get these bullets. Wherever his bullets came from, I would read the same page. I learned from him just by mimicking what he had done. So what did this bullet that he came up with came from? Chapter three, page four. What was the original source of this? He taught me so much. I was always studying his copy and looking at the product itself. And so I said, the only way I'm going to beat Gene is to out-research him and I made my piece longer and with the value added, I'll show you the sample later, I was able to beat him but it was really his package too in a way because I learned the technique just from looking at his bullets and seeing my bullets were much weaker. He really taught me how to take research. You almost have to read a sentence and then think about it. Read another sentence. Is there any possible drop of juice I could squeeze from this orange to turn it into marketing enticement in some way? Don't let a single paragraph go by without just pausing, you don't just race through it, you want to give it a lot of time and think about each thing you're looking at, can this be used in some way to make my mailing strong? And doing this process, Gene, just a mechanics of it, Gene would, told me once that, that's what I got to know, he said that he would just underline the book.

Readishive studies have shown that when you arrange your copied locks in a fairly logical sequence and the persuasion equation is a very good example of logic and the logical sequence of how people, you know, is solving a problem of mine. It's what is the promise to do it? What is the proof? It's a very logical sequence and putting something in the logical sequence makes in effect readership much more easy. If you're reading something about do you have this problem and then you go to the proposition and then you come back to the proof and you come back, if you're bound all over the place, it's going to be very hard for the reader to get very comfortable with you. You want to sort of make it like that grief shoot that many copywriters talk about. There's one paragraph gently, nicely flows right into the next one and you who approve copy should look for that. You know, you don't want speed bumps. If you're feeling a little speed bump along your, as you're reading the copy, there's something wrong. There shouldn't be a speed bump. It should flow like silk and the way to do it, the first step is to get all of your thoughts in logical sequence. So you put something in the introduction, you put something later and you kind of figure out what is a, it doesn't have to be pure logic, it just, what's a sensible way to organize all of this. So that your thoughts start to flow in a comfortable pattern with one another. And so that's how I organize my notes. I then, since it's on the computer, I will cut and paste and the introductory section now has five paragraphs I've cut and pasted. That will go into another document called introduction. Then I'll work on that to polish it and my introduction will be done and then the next piece and so on. So I, I, I, as I do all my research, which is in just kind of random order as I'm coming up with research ideas, but then I categorize them with my little handwritten notes on the left and I will cut and paste so that all similarly categorized entries are now in the same document and that's just a matter of figuring out the proper sequence for them.

## [42:31 - 45:32]

And that will make your writing flow very smoothly. It's a very, very thorough process. And one thing that I found is valuable. I heard about this service on the Internet. It's called CopyTalk, C-O-P-Y-T-A-L-K dot com. And what they will do is you can call up and make a record. Let me be, I have the Deluxe Service, I think it's $100 a month or something like that. And it's unlimited dictation for $100 a month. It's like you have your own personal dictation. What they do is they take your recording, they send it over to India where people type it up on the, and an email right back to you. So it's like you can have your own personal Steno assistant for $100 a month. And that even includes, I think, free 800 number calling when you call up. And it started really the service as a service for email where they, if you wanted to respond to your email and didn't want to take the time to type a message yourself if you're on the road, for example. So you call, you dictate an email, and if you want to check it before they send it out, you give them your email address, they'll send it back, probably usually the same day, the next morning, the latest. Usually very well done, but a few corrections I have to make. And then they will send it to you or the party that you've asked to send it. I always have it sent to me so I can proofread it, and then I send it out. I just put a different email in the address and forward it. It's a great service, though. They only let you dictate for about eight minutes at a time because so many writers, of all types of writers, started using this as their dictation service. It was really intended to be an email answering service, but there are many writers using it and you just dictate in eight minute segments. Or what I do is I dictate into a recorder and let it run for eight minute lengths, and then I have to make a few phone calls if I have a lot of research I'm dictating. But it saves a lot of keystrokes. Other copywriter productivity secrets. Best productivity secret I've ever learned anywhere is called the Pareto principle, or the 80-20 rule. One of the most successful executives that I've ever known had a sign over is this. Is this leading me to my main goal? And dozens of times during a day, he catch himself in an idle conversation or a meeting that wasn't going anywhere, and he'd see that sign and say, I've got to get back to the 20% of my activities, which yield 80% of my value to my company. And he tried to get everybody in the company thinking that way because this 80-20 rule is astounding in how universal it is.

## [45:32 - 48:35]

20% of the authors will sell 80% of the books. 20% of the customers will be responsible for 80% of your sales. 20% of your carpet gets 80% of your use. 20% of your wardrobe, you'll wear 80% of the time. And it may not always be exactly 20, 80. Sometimes it's 10 and 90 or 30, 70. But it's called the rule of the vital few and the trivial many. In any activity, there are a few vital factors which will give an outside result compared to all the other factors involved. And if you look for the 20% of your activities that are resulting in 80% of your success in life, you will be amazed at how you can then cut out everything else in the remaining 80% of your activities or much of it and put more time into the 20% that really yields the 80% of the results. For example, I as a writer feel the best, and I've learned this from Frank Betcher, a life insurance agent who wrote the book How I Raised Myself from Failure to Success in Selling. And he started applying this. He didn't know that that was the name of it. But he started realizing that the only time I really make any money is when I'm in front of a prospect selling insurance. So he started seeing how much time he was wasting, different meetings and phone calls and paperwork. And so he said, you know, I'm going to every day try to get myself in front of more prospects. And he realized the 20% of the activities that were making him 80% of his money and he just exploded his income. Now, I as a writer feel the best thing I can always put my time is in writing. And so I have what I've developed is my, what I call is my three hour day rule. If I don't, I try to, for the first moment I get up in the morning, try to start logging at least three hours doing the 20% that have gotten me the 80% of results in my life, which is writing. I'm a writer, so I should try to put it at least three hours a day in writing. That's what, for me, all the payoff is. So it's so easy to be seduced by answering email, going through all the papers on your desk, attending a meeting and planning a trip. You'll be really surprised at how many distractions are trying to pull you away from your 20%. And once you get conscious of that, you'll realize how important it is to try to get back on track. And in fact, if you have three hours a day, you can throw at any project, you can do almost anything, I've found. So I always try to, I'm kind of a grumpy bear. If the end of the day is coming, I haven't even logged a half hour of my three hours that I really wanted to log that day on what, for our little company, is our most successful activity. It's also the power of focus. We write our productivity secret number three, the power of focus. I mentioned about specialization. This applies to companies also. What a tragedy it was when IBM felt they were so strong in mainframes and when other types

## [48:35 - 51:38]

of computers came along, they tried to be everything for everybody, even in the computer business. And it's not possible. There are so many different types of computers that IBM lost so much money, untold billions of dollars, by trying to be everything to everybody in the computer business instead of sticking to their knitting, which was always the mainframes, and branching out from there for their customers. But many, many companies will make the same mistake. They think they will broaden out into an area where they're really not sure what they're doing, or they'll acquire a company that they really have no business acquiring, thinking that they can produce results because they're such whiz bangs at management and know how to market. But there are really three factors that apply in running a good business. And one is good sound management principles in managing your staff. The other one is marketing principles that you have to know. But the third is the knowledge of that specific industry. As good as I might think I am as a copywriter, I've never played golf in my life. So if I want to write an ad about golf, I've got a very steep learning curve to even begin to think about writing with John Colton on golf. I don't know about golf. John is a golfer. He's great at writing about golf among the series that he specializes in. And it really wouldn't make sense for me to try to do that because I'd have to learn years of knowledge that John has just absorbed in just by being a golfer and writing to golfers. So that's true of many different businesses. So often you hear of companies that merge and two years later they're unmerging. You hear so many major corporations that think they can learn anything because they know management and marketing, but they don't have the specific knowledge of that particular field they're asking for trouble. And so I advise you if you're thinking of getting into fields at a far field from your company, that's a great danger sign because if you don't know the ins and outs of that business, what's worked, the products that have sold over the past 50 years, the bombs that have taken place, the key contacts and business people who make that business really run that industry, it's a lot of knowledge that you're not going to catch up on very quickly. So how I budget my time on an assignment is 40% research, 40% writing, and 20% polishing. And believe it or not, it's normally for one of these really heavy duty book logs or mag logs where I'm giving a ton of information, a valuable information to my prospect, it'll take me a three month cycle. And that has always proven out okay for me because I have always wanted to really outperform anybody I'm going up against and I found very often the way to do that is with more time. When I was a younger copywriter, most of the direct mail that was out there was two page letters. And wanting to compete against that, I said, well, if I write a four page letter and fill it up with really good copy points, I will be able to outsell the two page letter. And it almost always worked that way. And then a lot of other people started making their letters longer and I was always sort

## [51:38 - 54:39]

of up the ante because I was one of the leading freelancers for many years. The trend went to eight page letters, I remember the first time I went to 12 and the clients were thinking I was crazy, how far can this go? Well, Cindy Burner may remember when my magologue was like, I think 40 pages shorter than the actual book that I was selling. The book was 120 pages and my magologue was 84 pages. And the magologue was better than the book, actually. But that's true. I mean, when you're talking to an interested marketplace and you're giving lots of good information away, we found these very long forms, I don't know where it will end, offline, but it costs a lot of money to produce these very long packages, 80 pages of print, 84 pages, takes a fair bit of color work and money to produce. So if you wanted to double that to 160 pages, it gets a little expensive offline. But online is no limit. It's sort of the world's cheapest printing press hooked up to the world's cheapest post office. So there's almost no limit, which is why I believe that the future of direct marketing really is online because the expenses are so, of marketing are so low. We have lots of problems, of course, with spam. But that's why I was saying before, if you can make your easings, other communications, really content rich so that people love to open them and again, the principle of that, making your first contact, you're advertising, even though it's not really advertising, you're easing and advertising, it's your communications extremely valuable so that you always get them open. When you do then have a subtle sales pitch, you'll get a much higher response. You never want to get known as the easing that's always pitching me something new every day. Now another very good example of how to do it right in many different styles is Agora. If you sign up for Early to Rise or there are other easings, and I've heard rumors of the amounts of money that Agora is making from their online customers and I wouldn't even think they sound so astounding for the amount of money that I heard that you're making online. But if you look at their easings and sign up for a few, you'll see why. They have brilliant copy coming on a whole wide range of subjects and they do it really, really well. I at first didn't believe that because I always felt that, hey, if I'm on a roll, if it's, you know, 11 o'clock at night and I'm really rolling with my copy, I want to go, I'll go to 3 in the morning if it's just to keep this momentum going. But that kind of creates, unless you do that every night, which then it is a regular routine,

## [54:39 - 57:42]

if you try to do it, if you try to, two days later, do you write at 9 in the morning and you're just bouncing all over the lot with your daily schedule, even though you did have good momentum on that one night, it probably throws you off for several days after. I have since tried to put this into practice and it made a tremendous difference for me that I try to do my writing at the same time of day. And for me, it's the morning. Before going to bed at night, I'll say, great subconscious mind, I want to work on this too hard, just figure out an answer by the morning. And by the morning, I'm kind of brimming over, I didn't lose any sleep over, it's not that major intensity use of my subconscious mind. I just say, I'd like, if you can work it out overnight, let me wake up with the answer. And I usually do and I'll be, pour out like a faucet in the morning. Great productivity secret because in effect, while you're sleeping, your subconscious mind is doing millions of activities, it's keeping your heart going, you hold its cellular transactions. It can handle a little request for a headline very easily without waking you up. So that's a very, very effective technique. In effect, it's like you just gained another eight hours or if you sleep for six hours, whatever your sleeping time is, you've let your subconscious mind just be going through lots and lots of permutations and you've gained an extra six hours of productivity without even working at all. And so that's a formula that I used after Deepak told me about that. Get to work right away in the morning because after giving your subconscious mind an assignment the night before, wake up, try to get to your writing as soon as you can, and I became so much more productive when I started doing that. Part of that is you want to get to bed at a reasonable time so that you don't throw that whole momentum off because when you get up early and you're about to turn on that faucet, if you stayed up really late, you're very fatigued and it may not work as well. So try that if you're looking for a way to really increase your productivity. So I just covered that, productivity, double or sleep on it, then write on a rising. Try to bear in mind the pressure is your enemy and relaxation your friend. When athletes and writers choke, you know, what does it mean when a great athlete in the World Series, he's had the batting championship all year long and then he keeps striking and he bats, you know, 120 in the World Series and his whole team, this feels let down. What it normally means, I think it may be always means, is that the person is just trying so hard. It's not that he doesn't want to succeed in the World Series. Sometimes you can want to try so hard that you become rigid, that you become so fixated that you're almost, you feel a muscular tension in your body. And for an athlete, that's death because no muscle moves as freely and as flexibly as when it's loose and relaxed. So if you start cramping up with tension, you're not going to swing the bat as quickly or pitch with the same fluid motion that you would normally have. So what great athletes know, have learned, is a tip that we can all learn from, the most important skill you get when you're in a high pressure situation is you just have to figure out a way to relax.

## [57:42 - 1:00:07]

If you can do that, you'll do much better because channels are open when you're relaxed. And so when you're riding, you don't want to be really rigid. So deadlines will tend to make you feel more rigid. So when you're taking on an assignment, really try to get enough time to do it right. See, of course, your client is thinking, this is the term Papi Syndrome. I'll give him three months to do it. He's going to really start two weeks before it's due. So they sometimes don't want to give you so much time. But if they're really intelligent clients, as the ones I've had to pledge it to work with, they'll give you whatever time you say you need. They might not like that you're taking as much time, but most of all, they want the job done right. And you really want to give yourself the time to do it right. So you can avoid that pressure at the end of the cycle. And don't let it be the term Papi Syndrome. Really jump on it right away. Start doing the easy things fast. Research almost like a general pattern, the way he wanted to march his way up through Sicily and then through Italy. We don't stop the whole ground. We just advance, advance, advance, just in that very early stage. You're not trying to be a perfectionist. You're just trying to move this job as fast as you can. Perfection and polishing can come later. This is not the time to be perfect. You just want to run as fast as you can, just like General Patton slicing his way through the boot of Italy. In the early stages, you do want to resist perfectionism for the reason I mentioned. The best way to do this is to not put any pressure on yourself to divide the whole assignment into its five logical parts. Number one, you want to do your research. Don't even worry about getting brilliant headlines or solving the problem. And this is hard to do because it's creating anxiety that you've got this powerful control package that you have to beat. But just resist that. You just want to do a lot of research. Number two, you want to organize your notes, as I've said before, into logical sections. Number three, you want to come up with your main theme and prioritize your sales arguments accordingly. And then only then in step 40 you start writing, and then step five is polish, polish, polish. So if you try to come to a perfect polished headline before you, or in the process of when you're doing research, it's almost like trying to drive it to brakes on. You can't do it. You're trying to do too many things at once. So let each phase of the writing process have its own very nice time to flourish. You can't grow corn overnight. Each stage has the germination. It has to grow. It has to get sun. So that's what you should do with your writing assignments as well.
